content.text: Gathering requirements for a conversational AI system is fundamentally different from gathering requirements for a form-based application. Users do not fill in fields — they express intent through natural language, across multiple turns, with ambiguity, abbreviation, and context that spans the session. Eliciting those requirements demands structured techniques that capture not just what the system should do, but what it should understand, how it should respond when understanding fails, and whose communication style it should adapt to.
Why It Matters

Conversational AI features fail most often not because the model is inadequate but because requirements were never captured with enough fidelity. A team that defines "the assistant should answer customer questions" will eventually discover it cannot measure success, cannot agree on which questions are in scope, and cannot explain to stakeholders why the model refuses certain requests. The downstream costs are rediscovery work, prompt rewrites in production, and eroded trust.

Structured elicitation produces a specification that is testable, reviewable, and promotable to an evaluation harness. It forces stakeholders to confront edge cases before they become production incidents, and it gives the engineering team a concrete definition of "done".
Benefits
Turns vague product intuitions into testable acceptance criteria that an evaluation suite can directly verify.
Forces early alignment between product, engineering, and compliance on scope boundaries and refusal policies.
Surfaces persona-specific needs (novice versus expert users, mobile versus desktop, API consumers) that would otherwise emerge as bugs.
Produces an intent taxonomy that seeds the evaluation dataset and informs prompt engineering.
Creates a shared vocabulary across teams, reducing the ambiguity that leads to misaligned implementations.
Trade-offs

Thorough elicitation is front-loaded effort. It slows down the start of development, which creates organizational pressure to skip it. The risk is real: over-specified requirements can constrain the model unnecessarily, particularly for generative features where rigid scripting defeats the purpose of using an LLM.
The practical balance is to specify
boundaries
precisely (what the system must never do, what it must always do) and specify
behaviors
as examples rather than exhaustive rules. This leaves room for model generalization while giving the team clear acceptance criteria.
How It Works

Conversational requirements elicitation proceeds in four phases: stakeholder interviews to establish intent taxonomy and user personas; dialogue mapping to trace happy paths and known edge cases; adversarial probing to surface failure modes; and specification writing to consolidate findings into a reviewable document.
The
intent taxonomy

is the backbone. It categorizes every class of user request the system is expected to handle: transactional intents (book, cancel, update), informational intents (what, when, how much), navigational intents (find, show, go to), and meta-intents (repeat that, rephrase, escalate to human). Each intent class carries a set of representative utterances, the entities that must be extracted, the expected system action, and the fallback behavior when entities are missing.

Dialogue mapping extends the taxonomy to sequences. A single intent rarely appears in isolation; users refine, correct, and follow up. Mapping traces multi-turn flows: what happens when the user says "actually, make it tomorrow" after a booking, what context must the system retain, and when does the session state expire.

Adversarial probing is the phase most teams skip. It asks: what happens when a user sends a query that is grammatically valid but semantically out of scope? What happens with profanity, PII in the input, or instructions designed to override system behavior? These probes often reveal requirements that compliance and legal teams care about deeply but that were never mentioned in the initial interviews.
Architecture

The process flows from stakeholder interviews through dialogue mapping to edge-case elicitation, culminating in a conversation spec document. Supporting artifacts — the intent taxonomy, persona requirements, and spec template — feed into the corresponding phases and are validated through an iterative prototype-test-refine loop.
When to Use It

Apply full conversational requirements elicitation for any feature where natural language is the primary interface: chatbots, voice assistants, AI copilots inside enterprise applications, and agentic systems that accept free-form task descriptions. The investment is proportional to the cost of a wrong interpretation — a customer-facing support bot with millions of users justifies more thoroughness than an internal prototype.
New product features where no prior conversational spec exists.
Migrations from a rules-based dialog system to an LLM — the old system's intent tree is the starting taxonomy.
Any time a compliance review requires documented evidence of the intended behavior scope.
How to Implement
Step 1: Conduct Stakeholder Interviews

Run structured workshops with three stakeholder groups: business owners (what must the system achieve?), domain experts (what language do users actually use?), and customer support or QA teams (what breaks and confuses users today?). Document findings as intent cards — one per intent class.
Step 2: Build the Intent Taxonomy

# intent_taxonomy.py — schema for a single intent entry
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional

@dataclass
class EntitySlot:
    name: str                     # e.g. "departure_date"
    type: str                     # e.g. "date", "location", "free_text"
    required: bool = True
    prompt_if_missing: str = ""   # clarification question to ask

@dataclass
class IntentSpec:
    id: str                               # e.g. "book_flight"
    category: str                         # transactional | informational | navigational | meta
    description: str
    trigger_utterances: List[str]         # representative user phrases
    entities: List[EntitySlot]
    expected_action: str                  # what the system should do
    success_response_template: str        # expected response shape
    fallback_behavior: str                # if confidence low or entities missing
    out_of_scope_phrases: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    escalation_triggers: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

# Example entry
book_flight = IntentSpec(
    id="book_flight",
    category="transactional",
    description="User wants to reserve a flight",
    trigger_utterances=[
        "book a flight to New York",
        "I need a ticket to NYC tomorrow",
        "can you find me a flight on Friday?",
    ],
    entities=[
        EntitySlot("destination", "location", required=True,
                   prompt_if_missing="Where would you like to fly to?"),
        EntitySlot("departure_date", "date", required=True,
                   prompt_if_missing="What date are you departing?"),
        EntitySlot("return_date", "date", required=False),
    ],
    expected_action="search_flights(destination, departure_date, return_date)",
    success_response_template="Here are available flights to {destination} on {departure_date}.",
    fallback_behavior="Ask for missing required entity; offer date picker if date unclear.",
)
Step 3: Map Dialogue Flows

# dialogue_flow.yaml — multi-turn flow for book_flight intent
flow_id: book_flight_flow
intent: book_flight

turns:
  - turn: 1
    user: "Book me a flight to Paris"
    system_action: extract_entity(destination=Paris)
    system_response: "When would you like to depart?"
    missing_entities: [departure_date]

  - turn: 2
    user: "Next Monday"
    system_action: resolve_date(departure_date=next_monday)
    system_response: "Return trip?"
    context_retained: [destination, departure_date]

  - turn: 3
    user: "No, one way"
    system_action: search_flights(destination=Paris, departure_date=next_monday)
    system_response: "Here are one-way flights to Paris on [date]..."

edge_cases:
  - description: user changes destination mid-flow
    trigger: "actually, make it London"
    expected: overwrite destination, retain departure_date, confirm change

  - description: date is in the past
    trigger: "last Tuesday"
    expected: clarify that date is past, ask for future date

  - description: ambiguous city name
    trigger: "Portland" (OR or ME?)
    expected: ask clarifying question with both options
Examples
Conversation Spec Template

# Conversation Spec: [Feature Name]

## Scope
- **In scope**: [list intent categories and domains]
- **Out of scope**: [explicit exclusions]
- **Escalation to human**: [conditions]

## Personas
| Persona       | Communication style   | Context          |
|---------------|-----------------------|------------------|
| Novice user   | Step-by-step guidance | Consumer mobile  |
| Power user    | Brief, shortcut-aware | Desktop web      |
| API consumer  | Structured JSON output| Programmatic     |

## Intent Inventory
| Intent ID       | Category       | Priority | Owner    |
|-----------------|----------------|----------|----------|
| book_flight     | transactional  | P0       | travel   |
| flight_status   | informational  | P0       | travel   |
| cancel_booking  | transactional  | P1       | travel   |

## Acceptance Criteria (per intent)
### book_flight
- GIVEN user provides destination + date THEN system returns >=1 flight option
- GIVEN missing departure date THEN system asks exactly one clarifying question
- GIVEN past date THEN system refuses and prompts for future date
- GIVEN ambiguous city THEN system disambiguates before searching

## Constraints
- Must never reveal internal tool names in responses
- Must not store PII beyond the session
- Response latency P95 <= 3 seconds

## Fallback Policy
- Low-confidence intent: "I'm not sure I understood. Could you rephrase?"
- Repeated failure: offer transfer to human agent
- Out-of-scope: "I can only help with [scope]. Is there something in that area?"
Persona-Based Requirements Extraction Script
Real-World Example

A financial services firm building an AI assistant for retail investors ran a two-week elicitation sprint. Stakeholder interviews with advisors uncovered three persona types: hands-off investors who wanted plain-language summaries, active traders who wanted precise numerical data and minimal prose, and compliance officers who needed every response to include a disclaimer.

Dialogue mapping revealed that "how is my portfolio doing?" — the most common intent — actually mapped to five distinct sub-intents depending on context: absolute return, relative-to-benchmark, recent volatility, largest position, and rebalancing suggestion. Without this mapping, the first prototype answered only absolute return and appeared unhelpful for the other four cases.

Adversarial probing uncovered a critical gap: users frequently asked about specific stocks they did not own. The spec had no guidance for this case. After stakeholder discussion, the team added an explicit out-of-scope rule: the assistant only discusses assets in the user's current portfolio, and redirects other queries to a research tab.
Common Mistakes
Specifying Only the Happy Path

Teams that document only what the system should do in ideal conditions leave all edge-case behavior to the model's defaults. The model may hallucinate a plausible-sounding answer, invent an action, or silently succeed in a way that violates a business rule. Every intent spec must include at least three edge cases.
Conflating Trigger Phrases with Intent Definitions

An intent is not a list of phrases; it is a semantic class. Specifying "book a flight" and "reserve a seat" as two separate intents leads to duplicated logic and inconsistent behavior. Group by meaning, not surface form, and use trigger phrases only as evaluation examples.
Excluding Technical Stakeholders from Interviews

Engineers know what is technically feasible and what context is available at inference time. A spec written without engineering input often requests entity extraction from data the system does not have access to, or assumes real-time lookups that would exceed latency budgets.
Best Practices
Use the Given/When/Then format for acceptance criteria — it maps directly to evaluation test cases.
Prioritize intents (P0/P1/P2) so the team knows what to evaluate first and what can be deferred.
Capture at least five real user utterances per intent from actual user research, not invented examples.
Version the conversation spec alongside the codebase so spec and implementation stay in sync.
Run a dedicated adversarial session with a red team before finalizing the spec — not as an afterthought.
Performance & Scalability

A conversation spec grows linearly with the number of intents. For a system with 50+ intents, the spec document becomes unwieldy as a monolith. Structure it as a machine-readable registry (JSON or YAML) with a separate human-readable summary. This allows automated tooling to generate evaluation datasets from the spec and flag when code changes affect covered intents.
Use a schema validator (pydantic, JSON Schema) to enforce spec structure at authoring time.
Store specs in version control and review them as code — treat spec changes as a signal to re-run evaluations.
Auto-generate evaluation test cases from trigger_utterances + expected_action fields to keep the eval suite in sync with the spec.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Miro / FigJam — visual dialogue flow mapping with team annotation.
Botmock / Voiceflow — specialized tools for intent mapping and conversation design that export intent catalogs.
Pydantic — enforce spec schema; auto-generate JSON Schema for validation and documentation.
Notion / Confluence — host human-readable spec with inline acceptance criteria tables, linked to code PRs.
Pytest + custom fixtures — generate evaluation tests directly from the intent registry YAML.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strength
Weakness
Best for
Structured intent taxonomy
Machine-readable, eval-ready
Time-consuming for large scope
Production systems
User story mapping
Familiar to agile teams
Does not capture multi-turn dialogue well
Simple chatbots
Ad-hoc prototyping
Fast to start
No testable criteria; rewrites in production
Internal PoCs only
BDD specification
Given/When/Then maps to evals
Misses entity extraction detail
Complement to intent taxonomy
Security & Reliability

The elicitation process must explicitly surface security-relevant requirements that might otherwise remain implicit. This includes: what PII the system may receive and must not store; what instructions users must not be able to inject through conversation (prompt injection scope); what system context must remain confidential; and which intents require authentication verification before action.
Include a dedicated "adversarial behavior" intent category covering jailbreaks, data exfiltration attempts, and scope violations.
Specify escalation triggers explicitly: what conditions cause the system to stop responding and defer to a human.
Document authentication requirements per intent — transactional intents typically require higher assurance than informational ones.
Mental Model

Think of a conversation spec as a contract between product and engineering, written in terms the model can be evaluated against. It does not dictate the exact words the model uses — that is the model's job — but it defines the semantic space the model must operate within, the entities it must extract, the actions it must take, and the boundaries it must never cross. Every sentence in the spec should be traceable to a test case.
Key Takeaways
Build an intent taxonomy as the backbone of the spec — categorize by semantic class, not by surface phrase.
Map multi-turn dialogue flows to capture context retention, correction handling, and session expiry requirements.
Run adversarial probing as a dedicated phase — not an afterthought — to surface out-of-scope and security requirements.
Document requirements per persona: novice, expert, mobile, and API consumers have genuinely different needs.
Write acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format so they translate directly into evaluation test cases.
Specify boundaries precisely; specify behaviors as examples — this preserves model generalization while maintaining testability.
Version the conversation spec alongside code and treat spec changes as a trigger for re-running evaluations.
